reasoning: The Federal Reserve recently slowed its quantitative tightening (QT) program, reducing the Treasury runoff cap to $5 billion per month starting April 1, 2025. Along with MBS runoff, the total pace is approximately $20 billion per month. In the current post-taper regime, the balance sheet trend is still downward but flatter, making positive weekly fluctuations (up to $4B) more competitive. For the 1-week horizon (May 18), the historical frequency of week-over-week increases is ~25-30%, thus a 0.26 probability is assigned. Over longer horizons, the baseline decreases steadily, making normal organic increases less likely; however, uncertainty heavily increases due to the possibility of a policy shift. Several economists forecast QT ending entirely by mid-to-late 2025, which would stabilize the balance sheet, and there remain tail risks of liquidity injections (e.g., related to debt ceiling resolutions). Following the explicit meta-instruction that long-term forecasts should present higher uncertainty (closer to 0.5), I scale my probabilities progressively upward from 0.26 at the 1-week horizon up to 0.48 for the 6-month horizon.
